section.title: The Birth of Jesus
section.sectionRationale: Having established Jesus' royal line, Matthew now tells how this child entered the world: not by the ordinary course of the genealogy but by the Holy Spirit, born of a virgin. The scene turns on Joseph, a righteous man wrestling with a crisis, and on an angel's word that unveils who this child is and what He has come to do.
section.thesis: The child conceived of the Holy Spirit and born of the virgin Mary is Jesus, the one who saves His people from their sins, and Immanuel, God with us, so that in His birth the promises of God and the very presence of God come to dwell among His people.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Mary's pregnancy and Joseph's dilemma; the angel's revelation of the child's name and mission; the fulfillment of Isaiah's prophecy of Immanuel; and Joseph's quiet obedience.

verses.text: This is how the birth of Jesus Christ came about: His mother Mary was pledged in marriage to Joseph, but before they came together, she was found to be with child through the Holy Spirit.
Because Joseph her husband was a {{dikaios|righteous}} man and was unwilling to disgrace her publicly, he resolved to divorce her quietly.

units.label: The angel's word
units.note: you are to give Him the name Jesus

verses.text: But after he had pondered these things, an angel of the Lord appeared to him in a dream and said, “Joseph, son of David, do not be afraid to embrace Mary as your wife, for the One conceived in her is from the Holy Spirit.
She will give birth to a Son, and you are to give Him the name {{iesous|Jesus}}, because He will save His people from their sins.”

units.label: To fulfill what was spoken
units.note: the virgin will be with child

verses.text: All this took place to fulfill what the Lord had said through the prophet:
“Behold, the {{parthenos|virgin}} will be with child and will give birth to a son, and they will call Him {{emmanouel|Immanuel}}” (which means, “God with us”).

units.label: Joseph obeys
units.note: he did as the angel commanded

verses.text: When Joseph woke up, he did as the angel of the Lord had commanded him and embraced Mary as his wife.
But he had no union with her until she gave birth to a Son. And he gave Him the name Jesus.

dikaios.gloss: Righteous, just, upright. Joseph's righteousness is shown not in rigid punishment but in a desire to obey God while sparing Mary shame; his character sets the tone for the obedient, tender-hearted man God entrusts with His Son.

iesous.gloss: Jesus, the Greek form of the Hebrew Yeshua or Joshua, meaning the LORD saves. The name is a job description: He will save His people from their sins. The angel ties the name directly to the mission of rescue that the whole Gospel unfolds.

parthenos.gloss: Virgin, a young unmarried woman who has not known a man. Matthew cites the Greek of Isaiah 7:14, where parthenos renders the Hebrew almah; the conception by the Holy Spirit fulfills the sign, so that this child has no human father.

emmanouel.gloss: Immanuel, Hebrew for God with us. Matthew makes it the frame of his whole Gospel: the child is God present with His people at the start, and at the end the risen Jesus promises, I am with you always, to the end of the age.

basic.context: Mary and Joseph were pledged in marriage, a binding commitment stronger than a modern engagement, when Mary was found to be pregnant. Joseph, knowing the child was not his, faced an agonizing choice. As a righteous man he planned to end the marriage quietly, to spare Mary public shame, until an angel revealed the truth in a dream and told him the child's name and mission.
basic.hermeneutics: This is history told to reveal who Jesus is. Matthew centers the account on Joseph rather than Mary, because his aim is to show that Jesus is legally David's heir and truly God with us. The angel's explanation of the name Jesus, and the quotation of Isaiah, are the interpretive keys: they tell us not just what happened but what it means.
basic.originalLanguages: The name Jesus means the LORD saves, and the angel spells out why: He will save His people from their sins. The prophecy speaks of a virgin, a woman who has not known a man, and names the child Immanuel, God with us. These two names, Jesus and Immanuel, sum up the whole Gospel: God has come, and God has come to save.
basic.typology: Isaiah's sign of Immanuel, given in a moment of national crisis, finds its deepest meaning here. The presence of God that filled the tabernacle and temple now takes on flesh in a child. The greater Joshua has come to lead His people, not into a land, but out of the deeper bondage of sin.
basic.christ: This passage is entirely about Christ: conceived by the Spirit, born of a virgin, named Jesus because He saves, and called Immanuel because He is God with us. He is fully human, born of Mary, and truly divine, God dwelling among us. His mission is named before His birth: to save His people from their sins.
basic.god: God enters His own world. The Holy Spirit conceives the child; the Father sends the angel; the Son is God with us. God does not save from a distance but comes near, taking flesh, to rescue a people who could never rescue themselves. And He works through the quiet obedience of an ordinary righteous man.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 7:14 is the prophecy quoted, the virgin and Immanuel. Luke 1 tells the annunciation to Mary. Philippians 2 describes the Son taking human form. And Matthew 28:20 closes the Gospel with the risen Jesus promising to be with us always, echoing Immanuel.
basic.oneStory: The God who has been keeping His promises through the long genealogy now steps personally into the story. In a manger lies both the answer to Israel's hope and the presence of God Himself. His name tells us the whole plot: He has come to save His people from their sins, and He will be with them to the very end.

context.literary: The account flows directly from the genealogy: having listed the line down to Joseph, Matthew now explains the puzzling break at verse 16, how Jesus is Joseph's son legally yet not by natural descent. The scene is built around a dream, one of several in Matthew's infancy narrative, and around the first of Matthew's formula quotations, all this took place to fulfill.
context.historical: Jewish betrothal was a legally binding covenant; ending it required a formal divorce, and a betrothed woman found pregnant faced disgrace and, by the letter of the law, grave danger. Joseph's plan to divorce Mary quietly reflects both his integrity and his mercy. Naming the child was the father's act, so Joseph naming Jesus formally adopts Him into David's line.
context.canonical: The virgin birth guards two truths the whole New Testament proclaims: that Jesus is genuinely human, born of a woman, and genuinely the Son of God, conceived by the Spirit. Isaiah 7:14 is fulfilled, and the name Immanuel frames Matthew's Gospel from beginning, God with us, to end, I am with you always, Matthew 28:20.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal that Jesus is conceived by the Holy Spirit and born of the virgin Mary, that His name declares His saving mission, and that He is Immanuel, God dwelling with His people, fulfilling the word spoken through Isaiah.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The virgin conception is a unique, unrepeatable act of God, described, not prescribed. Joseph's obedient, merciful faith, however, models a response worth imitating: he believed God's word and acted on it at real personal cost. The enduring doctrine is who Jesus is and what He came to do.

debates.question: Does Isaiah 7:14 speak of a virgin, and how is it fulfilled in Jesus?

views.label: A true virgin conception fulfilling the prophecy fully in Christ
views.case: Matthew cites the Greek of Isaiah, which reads parthenos, virgin, and presents the conception by the Spirit as the complete, intended fulfillment; the sign of Immanuel finds its true depth only in the child who is actually God with us.

views.label: A near-term sign in Isaiah's day with ultimate fulfillment in Jesus
views.case: Isaiah's word had an initial reference to a child born in his own time as a sign to Ahaz, yet Matthew, guided by the Spirit, sees its fullest and final meaning in the virgin-born Christ.

debates.bearing: Either way Matthew reads Jesus as the true Immanuel; the New Testament rests the incarnation on this text, and the virgin birth safeguards both His full humanity and His deity.

debates.question: In what sense was Joseph righteous in wanting to divorce Mary quietly?

views.label: Righteousness expressed as merciful restraint
views.case: Joseph honored the law's seriousness about apparent unfaithfulness yet refused to expose Mary to public shame, showing a righteousness that longs to do right while tempering justice with compassion.

views.label: Righteousness as reverent reluctance before a holy mystery
views.case: On this reading Joseph suspected something of God at work and, unwilling to presume, sought to step aside quietly; his righteousness is a humble hesitance before what he did not yet understand.

debates.bearing: Both portraits commend a righteousness that is neither harsh nor lax, and both are answered by the angel, who tells Joseph the truth and calls him to take Mary as his wife.

study.originalLanguages: The account hangs on names. Iesous, Jesus, is the Greek Yeshua, the LORD saves, and the angel makes the name a mission statement: He will save His people from their sins. The prophecy names a parthenos, a virgin, and calls the child Emmanouel, Immanuel, which Matthew himself translates, God with us. Joseph is dikaios, righteous, a man whose uprightness bends toward mercy. Together the names announce the Gospel in miniature: God has come near, and He has come to rescue.

typology.type: Immanuel, the sign of God's presence
typology.fulfillment: The presence that filled tabernacle and temple now dwells bodily in Jesus, God with us, John 1:14, and remains with His people always, Matthew 28:20.
As Joshua led Israel into the land, Jesus leads His people out of the bondage of sin into salvation, the deliverance His very name proclaims.
The prophesied child conceived apart from a human father, born to be the saving presence of God among His people.

study.christ: The whole passage is a portrait of Christ in two names. He is Jesus, for He will save His people from their sins, and He is Immanuel, God with us. Matthew insists on both His true humanity, conceived and carried by Mary, born as a real child, and His true deity, conceived by the Holy Spirit, God taking flesh. His saving work is announced before He draws a breath: not merely to teach or to reign, but to save, and to save from the deepest bondage, sin itself. In Him God has come near to do for us what we could never do for ourselves.
study.god: Here the living God steps into His own creation to save. The Holy Spirit conceives the child, the Father sends His messenger, and the Son is God with us in the flesh. God does not remain aloof from human misery; He comes down, is born of a woman, and takes a name that binds Him to rescuing sinners. And He accomplishes His purpose through the humble obedience of Joseph, showing that the great God works His salvation in and through the quiet faithfulness of ordinary people.

crossReferences.note: The prophecy Matthew quotes: the virgin will conceive and bear a son called Immanuel.
The angel's annunciation to Mary, complementing Matthew's focus on Joseph.
The Son, being God, takes on human likeness, the theology behind Immanuel in the flesh.
The risen Jesus promises, I am with you always, closing the frame of God with us opened here.

study.oneStory: The long story of promise takes its most astonishing turn: God Himself enters the line He has been preserving. The child in Mary's womb is the answer to Israel's hope and the presence of God among us. His name is His mission, to save His people from their sins, and His title is His nearness, God with us. From this cradle the whole Gospel unfolds, and its final promise, I am with you always, simply carries this birth to its end.

quiz.q: How was Mary found to be with child?

options.text: Through the Holy Spirit, before she and Joseph came together.
options.note: Verse 18 states plainly that the conception was through the Holy Spirit.

options.text: By a miracle of God, in a way Joseph could not explain.
options.note: True in effect, but the text names the Holy Spirit specifically as the agent of conception.

options.text: After she and Joseph were fully married and living together.
options.note: The text says it was before they came together, during betrothal.

options.text: By ordinary means, and the angel later blessed the child.
options.note: The conception was expressly not by ordinary means but through the Holy Spirit.

quiz.q: Why is the child to be named Jesus?

options.text: Because He will save His people from their sins, as the name the LORD saves declares.
options.note: The angel ties the name directly to His saving mission in verse 21.

options.text: Because He will be a great teacher and leader of Israel.
options.note: He is teacher and King, but the angel names His mission specifically as saving from sins.

options.text: Because it was a common family name to be passed down.
options.note: The name is chosen by God for its meaning, not merely as a family custom.

options.text: Because He will free Israel from Roman rule.
options.note: The salvation named is from sins, not from political oppression.

quiz.q: What does the name Immanuel mean, and why does Matthew stress it?

options.text: God with us; it declares that in Jesus, God Himself has come to dwell among His people.
options.note: Matthew translates it and frames his Gospel with it, ending on I am with you always.

options.text: God saves us; it points to the rescue Jesus brings.
options.note: That is the meaning of Jesus, not Immanuel; Immanuel means God with us.

options.text: God is powerful; it emphasizes divine might over enemies.
options.note: The name speaks of God's presence with us, not primarily His power over foes.

options.text: God is far above; it stresses His distance and holiness.
options.note: The point is the opposite: God is with us, near, not distant.

quiz.q: How does Joseph respond to the angel's revelation?

options.text: He obeys, taking Mary as his wife and naming the child Jesus.
options.note: Verses 24-25 show his full, obedient response to God's word.

options.text: He believes but delays acting until the child is born.
options.note: He did act at once on waking; he simply had no union with her until the birth.

options.text: He obeys reluctantly, still planning a quiet divorce.
options.note: He abandoned the divorce plan and embraced Mary as his wife.

options.text: He refuses and sends Mary away as first intended.
options.note: The opposite: he obeyed the angel and took Mary as his wife.

quiz.q: What do the two names Jesus and Immanuel together teach about the child?

options.text: That God has come near in the flesh, and He has come to save His people from their sins.
options.note: Immanuel names His presence, Jesus names His mission; together they sum up the Gospel.

options.text: That He is a righteous man specially blessed by God.
options.note: He is more than a blessed man; the names declare Him God with us and the Savior.

options.text: That He is a prophet sent to announce God's coming kingdom.
options.note: He is more than a prophet who announces God; He is God with us who saves.

options.text: That He is only a symbol of God's presence, not truly divine.
options.note: Immanuel means God actually with us; the conception by the Spirit marks true deity.

questions: Joseph was a righteous man whose righteousness bent toward mercy. Where might God be calling you to hold both truth and compassion in a difficult situation?
The angel told Joseph not to be afraid to take Mary as his wife. What fear is God calling you to face in obedience, trusting His word over your own understanding?
Jesus means the LORD saves, and He saves His people from their sins. How does naming your deepest need as sin, rather than mere circumstance, change what you look to Jesus for?
Immanuel means God with us. In what part of your life this week do you most need to remember that God is not distant but near?
Joseph quietly obeyed at real cost, and God accomplished salvation through his faithfulness. Where is God inviting you to trust that your ordinary obedience matters in His larger purposes?

translationNotes.note: Jesus renders the Hebrew Yeshua, the LORD saves; the BSB footnote makes the wordplay explicit, and the angel builds the mission on the meaning of the name.
The quotation is Isaiah 7:14. Matthew follows the Greek reading virgin, parthenos, and adds his own translation of Immanuel as God with us.
Had no union with her renders a Hebrew idiom, literally he did not know her, describing that the marriage was not consummated until after Jesus was born.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 1:18-25. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; note and line-break markers were skipped. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
